resources.string: Japanese pronunciation is relatively straightforward compared to English, but there are some key differences in rhythm and accent that you should know.
The vowels (A, I, U, E, O) are always pronounced the same way. Unlike English where "a" can sound different in "apple" and "cake", Japanese vowels are consistent.
Japanese is a mora-timed language. This means each kana (including the small symbols) takes up roughly the same amount of time to say. This gives Japanese its steady rhythm.

Instead of a "stress accent", Japanese uses "sokuon" (small っ). This indicates a slight pause before the next consonant, effectively doubling it.
Example: きて (kite - come)
vs
きって (kitte - stamp).
While English uses "stress accent" (loudness), Japanese uses "pitch accent" (high or low tone). The meaning of a word can change depending on which syllable is high or low.

Changing the length of a vowel can completely change the meaning of a word. 

Example: 
おばさん (obasan) - Aunt 
おばあさん (obaasan) - Grandmother

The kana "ん" is the only consonant that can stand alone. Its pronunciation changes depending on what follows. It can sound like "n", "m", or "ng". For example, when followed by "b" or "p", it is often pronounced as "m".
